edges.target: Respiratory Epithelial Infection and Airway Inflammation

nodes.description: Nirsevimab is a long-acting recombinant monoclonal antibody against the RSV fusion (F) protein, given as a single injection for passive immunoprophylaxis of infants before the RSV season. By neutralizing the F protein it blocks viral entry/fusion, preventing medically attended RSV lower respiratory tract infection — a broad-spectrum-preventative (passive-immunity) approach.

evidence.snippet: monoclonal antibody to the RSV fusion protein that has an extended half-life.

nodes.description: RSV infects ciliated airway epithelial cells, and its fusion protein drives cell-cell fusion producing multinucleated syncytia. Epithelial injury, sloughing of necrotic epithelium, mucus hypersecretion, and an inflammatory cellular infiltrate obstruct the small airways (bronchiolitis), causing air trapping and ventilation-perfusion mismatch. The host inflammatory and immune response contributes substantially to disease severity. In older and high-risk adults the same infection can precipitate cardiopulmonary decompensation.
nodes.id: Respiratory Epithelial Infection and Airway Inflammation

evidence.reference_title: Respiratory Syncytial Virus: Infection, Detection, and New Options for Prevention and Treatment.
evidence.snippet: Respiratory syncytial virus (RSV) infection is a significant cause of hospitalization of children in North America and one of the leading causes of death of infants less than 1 year of age worldwide, second only to malaria.

evidence.reference_title: Respiratory Syncytial Virus: Infection, Detection, and New Options for Prevention and Treatment.
evidence.snippet: Compounding the burden of acute RSV infections is the exacerbation of preexisting chronic airway diseases and the chronic sequelae of RSV infection.

evidence.reference_title: Respiratory syncytial virus infection in elderly and high-risk adults.
evidence.snippet: Respiratory syncytial virus (RSV) is an increasingly recognized cause of illness in adults.
